3: Algae are increasingly considered a promising feedstock for bio- energy production, because they are highly productive, can be grown on marginal land in fresh, brackish, or even saline water, and they do not directly compete with food production (Benemann and Oswald, 1996). Despite this, there are significant environmen- tal and financial challenges that must be overcome before algae cultivation and conversion can be made sustainable (Clarens et al., 2010; Lundquist et al., 2010). One particularly important

challenge is procurement of low cost, low energy-intensiveness nutrients, most notably nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P). It has been demonstrated that nutrient procurement is one of the most energy intensive processes and can constitute up to 50% of energy consumption during algae cultivation when fertilizers are used (Clarens et al., 2010; Stephenson et al., 2010). On the other hand, nutrient-rich wastes from animal agriculture pose significant envi- ronmental challenges such as regional eutrophication and global warming potential. As an example, water quality impairment in the Chesapeake Bay (the largest estuary in the United State) has been partially attributed to excess runoff and discharge of nutri- ents from farms, of which 18% of N and 25% of P arises from animal wastes (Chesapeake Bay Foundation, 2004). Animal waste is also an increasing source of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, including methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20). The US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) estimates that GHG emissions from

animal wastes increased by almost 60% during the period between 1990 and 2009 (EPA, 2010). Therefore, there is significant interest in leveraging possible synergies between algae-derived energy production and animal waste management to simultaneously im- prove the environmental sustainability and economic attractive- ness of each individual system.

Open pond systems (OPSs) have been the best studied configu- ration for large scale algae cultivation. Several previous reports have estimated the environmental impacts and economic costs of algae-derived energy sources produced in OPSs, and this configura- tion is generally considered to be the most practical option for near term deployment of algae-to-energy systems (Clarens et al., 2011; Lundquist et al., 2010). Use of partially treated wastewaters as nutrient source for mass algae culture in OPSs has been shown to improve the environmental performance of algae-to-energy sys- tems by reducing virgin fertilizer consumption and also offsetting energy requirements for N and P removal in a municipal wastewa- ter treatment plant (Clarens et al., 2010; Lundquist et al., 2010; Pittman et al., 2011). Both benefits increase with increasing efflu- ent N and P concentrations; however, there have been few compre- hensive LCA studies to analyze the use of nutrient-rich animal wastes as nutrient source. This is surprising given the much larger N and P concentrations in animal wastes and several previous re- ports which have demonstrated bench-scale algae growth on these media (Markou and Georgakakis, 2011).

The so-called "algae turf scrubber" system (ATS) is one alterna- tive to OPSs for algae cultivation at large sale. This system has been the topic of several previous technical assessments, which have documented its ability to produce sustained algae yields of up to 40 g dry solids (DS)/m2-day. ATS systems have also been shown to mediate efficient nutrient removal; up to 2,500 kg N/ha/yr and 490 kg P/ha/yr, from river water, agricultural runoff, animal man- ure effluent, or industrial wastewater (Kebede-Westhead et al., 2003; Mulbry et al., 2008a,b). Pizarro et al. (2006) evaluated the economic feasibility of using ATS to treat dairy manure effluents on large dairy farms (1000 cows). This assessment did not include a full scale environmental life cycle assessment (LCA), and it only accounted for one of several possible financial benefits associated with use of an ATS system; namely, efficient recycling of nutrients into algae biomass such that purchase of N and P is not required for sustained algae cultivation. Some additional financial benefits of these systems could include: sale of algae-derived electricity; sale of N, P, and/or CO2 credits in a regional or national trading market; and sale of post-digestion residuals as nutrient-rich soil amend- ment (i.e., fertilizer). It is necessary to account for all of these ele- ments within a life cycle costing (LCC) framework to fully understand and anticipate the financial performance of farm-scale ATS systems. It is also necessary to assess the environmental per- formance of ATS systems, using a life cycle assessment (LCA) framework, such that full information is available for sustainability decision making about possible integration of algae farming and nutrient management.

This study was conducted with three specific objectives: (1) as- sess the relative environmental performance of OPS and ATS sys- tems for manure nutrient management on small dairy farms using LCA; (2) assess the relative financial performance of OPS and ATS systems for manure nutrient management on small dairy farms using LCC; and (3) compare the overall environmental and financial performances of OPS and ATS systems with two conven- tional manure nutrient management strategies (anaerobic diges- tion and on-farm land application) to evaluate which strategy is optimally sustainable. We focused on small farms (i.e., 100 cows) because this is roughly the size of a typical farm in the Southeast- ern USA (Groover, 1998), and it is expected that the growing nutri- ent trading program in this region could increase the attractiveness of these systems for local farmers.

It has been demonstrated that nutrient procurement is one of the most energy intensive processes and can constitute up to 50% of energy consumption during algae cultivation when fertilizers are used (Clarens et al., 2010; Stephenson et al., 2010). On the other hand, nutrient-rich wastes from animal agriculture pose significant envi- ronmental challenges such as regional eutrophication and global warming potential. As an example, water quality impairment in the Chesapeake Bay (the largest estuary in the United State) has been partially attributed to excess runoff and discharge of nutri- ents from farms, of which 18% of N and 25% of P arises from animal wastes (Chesapeake Bay Foundation, 2004). Animal waste is also an increasing source of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, including methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20). The US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) estimates that GHG emissions from

animal wastes increased by almost 60% during the period between 1990 and 2009 (EPA, 2010). Therefore, there is significant interest in leveraging possible synergies between algae-derived energy production and animal waste management to simultaneously im- prove the environmental sustainability and economic attractive- ness of each individual system.

Open pond systems (OPSs) have been the best studied configu- ration for large scale algae cultivation. Several previous reports have estimated the environmental impacts and economic costs of algae-derived energy sources produced in OPSs, and this configura- tion is generally considered to be the most practical option for near term deployment of algae-to-energy systems (Clarens et al., 2011; Lundquist et al., 2010). Use of partially treated wastewaters as nutrient source for mass algae culture in OPSs has been shown to improve the environmental performance of algae-to-energy sys- tems by reducing virgin fertilizer consumption and also offsetting energy requirements for N and P removal in a municipal wastewa- ter treatment plant (Clarens et al., 2010; Lundquist et al., 2010; Pittman et al., 2011). Both benefits increase with increasing efflu- ent N and P concentrations; however, there have been few compre- hensive LCA studies to analyze the use of nutrient-rich animal wastes as nutrient source. This is surprising given the much larger N and P concentrations in animal wastes and several previous re- ports which have demonstrated bench-scale algae growth on these media (Markou and Georgakakis, 2011).

The so-called "algae turf scrubber" system (ATS) is one alterna- tive to OPSs for algae cultivation at large sale. This system has been the topic of several previous technical assessments, which have documented its ability to produce sustained algae yields of up to 40 g dry solids (DS)/m2-day. ATS systems have also been shown to mediate efficient nutrient removal; up to 2,500 kg N/ha/yr and 490 kg P/ha/yr, from river water, agricultural runoff, animal man- ure effluent, or industrial wastewater (Kebede-Westhead et al., 2003; Mulbry et al., 2008a,b). Pizarro et al. (2006) evaluated the economic feasibility of using ATS to treat dairy manure effluents on large dairy farms (1000 cows). This assessment did not include a full scale environmental life cycle assessment (LCA), and it only accounted for one of several possible financial benefits associated with use of an ATS system; namely, efficient recycling of nutrients into algae biomass such that purchase of N and P is not required for sustained algae cultivation. Some additional financial benefits of these systems could include: sale of algae-derived electricity; sale of N, P, and/or CO2 credits in a regional or national trading market; and sale of post-digestion residuals as nutrient-rich soil amend- ment (i.e., fertilizer). It is necessary to account for all of these ele- ments within a life cycle costing (LCC) framework to fully understand and anticipate the financial performance of farm-scale ATS systems. It is also necessary to assess the environmental per- formance of ATS systems, using a life cycle assessment (LCA) framework, such that full information is available for sustainability decision making about possible integration of algae farming and nutrient management.

This study was conducted with three specific objectives: (1) as- sess the relative environmental performance of OPS and ATS sys- tems for manure nutrient management on small dairy farms using LCA; (2) assess the relative financial performance of OPS and ATS systems for manure nutrient management on small dairy farms using LCC; and (3) compare the overall environmental and financial performances of OPS and ATS systems with two conven- tional manure nutrient management strategies (anaerobic diges- tion and on-farm land application) to evaluate which strategy is optimally sustainable. We focused on small farms (i.e., 100 cows) because this is roughly the size of a typical farm in the Southeast- ern USA (Groover, 1998), and it is expected that the growing nutri- ent trading program in this region could increase the attractiveness of these systems for local farmers.
9: Four manure nutrient management strategies were evaluated using complementary LCA and LCC frameworks. These include: Scenario REF (reference), where manure waste is managed using conventional on-farm land application; Scenario AD (anaerobic digestion), where manure waste is subjected to anaerobic digestion for biogas production and nutrient recovery; and Scenarios OPS and ATS, where post-digestion liquid waste is used as nutrient source for algae cultivation in an open pond system (OPS) or algal turf scrubber (ATS), respectively, and resulting algal biomass is co- digested with dairy waste for enhanced bioenergy production. These scenarios are depicted schematically in Fig. 1. In all cases, nutrient concentrations and other characteristics of dairy manure were based on Van Horn et al. (1994). On-farm waste application (for scenarios REF and AD) was assumed to occur during the crop growing season, which is March through September in the South- eastern USA. Algae cultivation was assumed to occur from March through November (Mulbry et al., 2008; Pizarro et al., 2006). Wastes generated and collected outside of the biomass growing season were stored on-farm in lagoons.

Systems boundaries for all four scenarios were "cradle to gate" for dairy manure management systems, encompassing all pro- cesses associated with dairy manure treatment; including collec- tion and storage of the waste, extraction of raw resources for production of required energy/material inputs, production of bio- electricity for on-farm use or sale to the local grid, and disposi- tion/export of residual materials. All facilities and processes associated with cow breeding and manure collection were ex- cluded from analysis, because they are held in common for all eval- uated scenarios and it was assumed that these items would already be in place at all farms trying to decide among the four evaluated scenarios. Environmental impacts associated with construction of capital infrastructure and equipment were calculated by multiply- ing required material inputs by their corresponding database im- pact factors, as obtained from the ecoinvent database (Weidema, 2007). These quantities were divided by an assumed 20-years use- ful life to facilitate direct comparison with annual impacts arising from operations in each scenario. The functional unit was defined as management of as much manure is produced on a 100-cow farm, to account for the multiple functions of the various systems, including: nutrient recycling, biomass production (corn in REF and AD, or algae in OPS and ATS), energy production, and creation of soil amendment.

LCA models accounted for three types of environmental im- pacts: net energy use (EN), net eutrophication potential (EUT), and net global warming potential (GWP). LCC models were con- structed using the LCA mass-flow models as basis (Figures S1, S2, and S3 of the SI). The outcome of the LCC analysis was computation of net present value (NPV) for each system, based on three types of cash flows: initial outlay, annual operating cost, and annual reve- nue. Initial outlay was the total capital investment required for purchase of materials and equipment, construction costs, and mis- cellaneous costs. Annual operating costs included energy, labor, and capital depreciation. Annual revenues accounted for five possi- ble sources, not all of which are achievable for a single scenario: (1) sale of corn (for REF and AD); (2) sale of biogas-derived bioelectric- ity (for AD, OPS, and ATS); (3) sale of N and P treatment credits, using the 80% of REF nutrient loadings as "baseline" and assuming that the nutrient loading reductions achieved by each "best management practice" (AD, OPS, and ATS) are eligible to be sold (Branosky et al., 2011; Maryland Department of Agriculture, 2008); (4) sale of carbon dioxide (CO2) credits within the national carbon market (for AD, OPS, and ATS) (Cap-and-Trade Program, 2012; Chicago Climate Change, 2011), which is the reduction of CO2 emission compared to scenario REF; (5) sale of post-digestion

1: The first step in evaluating the environmental and economic performances of each manure waste management scenario was to compute what nutrient masses are generated annually on a typ- ical 100-cow farm in the southeastern USA and then assess where these nutrients flow to in each evaluated farm waste management scenario. The magnitudes of these flows are summarized in Fig- ure S7 of the SI. A report from Van Horn et al. (1994) indicates that each cow excretes 2,578 kg of waste per year as feces and urine, roughly 80% of which is volatile material. Total nitrogen (TN) excretion is 108 kg per cow per year, and total phosphorus (TP) excretion is 20 kg per cow per year.

For REF, the manure slurry (10% DS by mass) is either used di- rectly for on-farm land application or stored for use during the next growing season, depending on the time of year. After N losses dur- ing storage and spreading (see SI Section 3) the biologically avail- able TN mass to be managed via on-farm land application is 2,710 kg per year per 100 cows.

For the AD scenario, the manure slurry (again 10% DS by mass) is collected into an anaerobic digester. Average effluent flow rate for a 100-cow farm is 6.9 n°/d, and digestion retention time is roughly 14 During digestion, 30% of TN and 60% of TP is seques- tered into the solid digestate. Post-digestion effluent is then trans- ferred to a storage tank for separation into solid and liquid streams via gravity induced settling and screw pressing. The solid stream

comprises 155,640 kg/yr, and it contains 30% of the post-digestion TN. The solids are shipped off-farm for use as soil amendment at a sale price of roughly $12/Mg. The liquid stream comprises 2,500 /yr, and it contains 70% of the post-digestion TN. The li- quid is either used directly for on-farm land application or stored for use during the next growing season, depending on the time of year. After N losses during storage and spreading (see SI Sec- tion 3.4), the biologically available TN mass to be managed via on-farm land application is 1,141 kg/yr per 100 cows

For the OPS and ATS scenarios, manure slurry is collected into an anaerobic digester and digested just the same as in the AD. The post-digestion slurry is then transferred to a storage tank for solid/liquid separation. The flow rates of solids and liquids, and also the respective TN concentrations in these streams, are the same as in AD. Solids are shipped off-farm, and storage N losses in the liquid stream are the same as in the AD scenario. Assuming 275 operational days per year (March through November) for algae cultivation, the post-digestion TN and TP masses give rise to 118 Mg of algae biomass per year in the OPS scenario and 153 Mg of algae biomass per year in ATS. The difference between these yields can be attributed to the difference of algae yield (ash free dry mass): 12.6 g/m ²-day in OPS versus 18.5 g/m2-day in ATS.

4: Direct land use is the land area required for manure manage- ment in each scenario; i.e., corn cultivation on undigested or di- gested manure waste for REF and AD, or the algae cultivation footprint for OPS and ATS operation. For the REF and AD scenarios, manure was applied at a rate meeting but not exceeding the nutri- ent requirements of the crop being grown in a particular soil based on the crop's nitrogen demand (agronomic N rate), with the assumption that this would also mediate complete TP consump- tion. The agronomic N rate was assumed to be 157 kg/ha TN on typical dairy farms in Virginia (Evanylo 2009). The total quantity of N available to plants in land-applied dairy manure, comprising inorganic N remaining after volatilization plus 25% of total organic

N, was 2,710 and 1,141 kg N/yr for REF and AD, respectively. Aver- age land use requirements for on-farm dairy manure management were 17.7 ha for REF and 7.4 ha for AD, accounting for 12% and 5% of the average farm size in this region (155 ha) (Groover, 1998), respectively.

Regarding the OPS and ATS scenarios, total N and P available from dairy manure after digestion are 6,722 kg N/yr and 788 kg P/yr, respectively. Mulbry et al. (2008) found that the recov- ery rate of P from dairy manure is up to 100%, greater than that of N; therefore, P is the limiting nutrient for algae cultivation using dairy wastes. The OPS and ATS systems evaluated in this study were thus sized to completely consume all of the available P. It was also necessary to account for the TN arising from co-digestion of the algae biomass with the dairy manure, since it had been pre- viously demonstrated that roughly half of the post-digestion algae nitrogen makes its way into the solid digestate (Anasruron et al., 2010; Sialve et al., 2009; Starman 2009). The OPS and ATS land area required to completely consume liquid digestate N were roughly 3.4 ha of OPS and 2.6 ha of ATS. Thus, both algae systems require less direct land use than REF and AD, and each will require no more than 3% of the average farm size in this region (155 ha) (Groover, 1998). This suggests that farms will not need to purchase addi- tional land to implement an algae-mediated nutrient management system.

6: Fig. 2 summarizes the environmental performances of all four evaluated nutrient management scenarios. From these results, AD, OPS, and ATS are environmentally preferable to REF. The three evaluated environmental impacts include eutrophication potential (EUT), net energy use, and net global warming potential (GWP) (based on GHG emissions). These are discussed in the following paragraphs.
7: On-farm land application (REF) constitutes the most widely- used manure management practice at present. But this methodol- ogy is problematic with respect to nutrient control for several reasons, most notably: 1) considerable N release during storage and land spreading, 2) significant runoff of N and P following waste

Fig. 2. LCA results for delivery of one functional unit in each manure management scenario. EN is energy use (in GJ/yr), GWP is global warming potential (in Mg CO2- eq/yr), and EUT is eutrophication potential in (kg PO4-eq/yr). For EUT and GWP, values greater than zero indicate net output from the system boundaries, values less than zero indicate net input across the system boundaries. application, and 3) the relatively short duration of the growing sea- son when waste can be applied makes it necessary for farms to de- vote large land areas to manure management (Van Horn et al., 1994). It was hypothesized that the improved nutrient control afforded by an AD, OPS, or ATS system could be an incentive for farms to improve their manure management strategy, especially in the face of increasingly stringent environmental regulations, but it was necessary to evaluate what EUT performance enhance- ment each system would deliver.

From Fig. 2, AD offers considerable EUT improvement over REF, and both algae systems offer dramatic EUT reductions compared to AD and REF. The EUT values for both OPS and ATS are roughly 325 kg PO4-eq/yr per functional unit, which is nearly seven times smaller than for AD (2162 kg PO4-eq/yr) and roughly nine times smaller than for REF (3024 kg PO4-eq/yr). For both algae cases (OPS and ATS), the nutrient-rich liquid digestate is recycled into the algae cultivation system instead of spread onto farmland. This essentially eliminates the N and P fluxes which would otherwise occur following land application of raw or digested manure waste, as in the REF and AD cases, respectively. The difference in EUT be- tween REF and AD reflects partial nutrient removal during diges- tion: roughly 30% of pre-digestion N and 60% of pre-digestion P are separated from the post-digestion liquid effluent via various processes.

10: <header>3.3.2. Net energy use (EN) </header>

It was expected that enhanced EUT performance may be the pri- mary reason for farms to consider alternatives to the REF manure waste management strategy. However, it was also hypothesized that use of AD, OPS, or ATS in place of REF could deliver additional environmental benefits. In particular, it was expected that addition of an energy-producing process could transform manure waste management from an economic and environmental liability into a means of generating additional farm revenue. In this vein, anaer- obic digestion seemed an obvious choice, since there is already some precedent for on-farm dairy waste digestion (Van Horn et al., 1994). Beyond this, it was hypothesized that addition of an algae cultivation system could simultaneously increase the amount of digestible biomass produced on-farm and improve nutrient management (Section 3.2.1). It has been previously dem- onstrated that algae exhibiting lipid contents less than 40% (including many native OPS and ATS crops) are unsuitable for bio- diesel production and are thus better leveraged via anaerobic digestion (Sialve et al., 2009). Thus, there was significant motiva- tion for exploration of on-farm manure waste management sys- tems coupling anaerobic digestion and algae cultivation, but it was unknown how energetically favorable these systems would be and how they would compare to each other.

From Fig. 2, AD, OPS, and ATS exhibit net energy production per FU. Only REF exhibits net energy consumption. From this, anaero- bic digestion must be an important differentiating factor, presum- ably because it mediates two types of energy production: direct energy in the form of bioelectricity, and indirect energy as solid dig- estate residuals, which can supplant commercial fertilizers. Regarding direct energy generation, for AD, only the manure slurry is digested to produce biogas. For OPS and ATS, manure slurry is co-digested with algae biomass. For a 100-cow farm, the amounts of volatile material digested in each system are 209,150 kg/yr for AD, 311,467 kg/yr for OPS, and 338,508 kg/yr for ATS. For OPS and ATS, algae biomass constitutes roughly 40% of total digestible biomass. The increased amount of volatile material for OPS and ATS would necessitate significant increases in digester capacity to maintain the same retention time as in AD. These increases (70-100%) were accounted for as increased materials usage in OPS and ATS. The amounts of CH4-derived bioelectricity produced per FU are 1,044 GJ/yr for AD, 1,662 GJ/yr for OPS, and 1,802 GJ/yr

for ATS, assuming digestion yields are 0.42 L CH4/g DS manure (ash free) and 0.49 L CH4/g DS algae (ash free). The algae methane yield could be an underestimate, since there is bench-scale evidence to suggest that this quantity increases in the presence of a carbon- rich digestion co-substrate (e.g., manure) (Yen and Brune, 2007); however, this possible impact could not be accounted for due to data scarcity.

Regarding indirect energy generation, the masses of solid dige- state produced per FU are 156, 232, and 256 Mg/yr in the AD, OPS, and ATS scenarios, respectively. Multiplying these by a bioavail- ability equivalency factor and the amount of energy required to produce commercial fertilizer (37.5 MJ/kg H12N3O4P and 62 MJ/kg urea) (Weidema, 2007), the energy offsets embodied by production of soil amendment are: 101 GJ/yr for AD, 189 GJ/yr for OPS, and 199 GJ/yr for ATS. REF produces only a small amount of energy pro- duction, this as an offset associated with production of corn silage:
1.1 GJ/ha per FU, assuming 0.36 MJ/kg corn.

Energy consumption was also evaluated for each nutrient man- agement strategy. Not surprisingly, REF requires the lowest energy consumption (199 GJ/yr per FU) because it involves the least num- ber of steps. Energy consumption in this scenario is mostly associ- ated with operation of manure spreading equipment, including upstream impacts associated with production of the necessary die- sel fuel.

Energy consumption for AD (301 GJ/yr per FU) was roughly 1.5 times that of REF. This is because land application of liquid dige- state consumes roughly half (61%) of the total energy required for the AD scenario, and this quantity is held in common between the AD and REF scenarios. The other main energy consuming pro- cess for AD is operation of the digester, which comprises 33% of overall AD energy consumption. Interestingly, annualized energy consumption for "upstream" production of AD infrastructure is nearly negligible, accounting for only 6% of annual AD energy con- sumption when divided by the digester's assumed useful life years).

Energy consumption for OPS (1,240 GJ/yr) and ATS (1,375 GJ/yr) was understandably greater than for either AD or REF. This is be- cause additional energy is required beyond what is consumed for operation of the anaerobic digester and application of the liquid digestate. The larger number of processes and material inputs re- quired for both algae cultivation scenarios makes it worthwhile to investigate the allocation of overall energy consumption in each scenario, as means to identify possible improvements.

From Table 1, OPS and ATS exhibit small difference in overall energy consumption (input) per FU, but allocation of energy use is very different between the two. The OPS system exhibits consid- erable balance among three principal energy-consuming entities: algae cultivation consumes 22% of the total, operation of the anaer- obic digester consumes 28%, and annualized upstream infrastruc- ture burdens accounts for 50%. In contrast, the ATS system is less balanced, with greater energy consumption for algae cultivation (62%), less consumption for operation of the anaerobic digester (28%), and much smaller annualized upstream infrastructure bur- dens (10%). Two especially interesting implications of energy use allocation include: (1) the balance between direct and indirect use; and (2) the extent to which improvement in selected sub- processes could improve overall energy bottom line. These are discussed in the following paragraphs.

Regarding direct versus upstream energy consumption, an algae farming entity is likely most interested in minimizing direct, on- farm electricity and heat consumption (e.g., for pumping, diges- tion, etc.), since these have an economic cost. In contrast, there is currently no mandatory mechanism for monetizing upstream en- ergy consumption (e.g., for production of virgin CO2 or manufac- ture of infrastructure materials). In Table 1, direct energy use for algae cultivation and digestion processes constitutes 50% of total energy consumption per FU in OPS and 90% in ATS. This may make OPS seemingly more desirable than ATS for an algae farming entity, together with the fact that the total amount of energy consumed per FU is also smaller in OPS than in ATS.

With respect to possible system improvements for each nutrient management scenario, it is desirable to focus design efforts on a small number of particularly energy intensive sub-processes. Thus, systems exhibiting "unbalanced" energy consumption (whereby a small number of sub-processes constitute the majority of overall energy consumption) may be most ripe for overall improvement. From Table 1, both OPS and ATS exhibit several key sub-processes, which, if improved, could register an appreciable reduction on overall energy consumption per FU. But ATS exhibits less balanced energy consumption compared to OPS. As such, a 50% improve- ment in ATS electricity usage alone (596 GJ) would mediate greater energy savings than a 25% improvement in energy intensiveness in OPS algae cultivation + digestion + OPS infrastructure (68 + 85 + 157 MJ = 310 Of course, not all sub-processes are equally likely to be improved. Of the items listed in Table 1, topics of interest for near-term improvement via decreased energy con- sumption include: electricity consumption, nutrient supply, and infrastructure burdens. These are discussed in the following paragraphs.

Electricity Consumption. Direct energy use will be a key factor in decision making about on-farm waste nutrient management, be- cause farms must pay for both heat and electricity. From Table 1, heat consumption is roughly the same in both algae systems (~30 GJ/FU), but electricity consumption varies dramatically: 363 GJ/FU for OPS versus 1,193 GJ/FU for ATS. Additional electricity consumption in ATS is not offset by additional electricity produc- tion, since both algae systems produce roughly the same amount of bioelectricity (1,700 GJ/FU). The difference in electricity con- sumption between OPS and ATS arises mostly from cultivation usage (55 GJ/FU in OPS versus 846 GJ/FU in ATS), since both sys- tems use about the same amount for anaerobic digestion. Cultiva- tion-phase electricity usage of ATS is primarily for pumping water up out of the sump to the head of the floway, which accounts for 47% of overall energy usage. Two ways to reduce pump electricity consumption include: increasing the efficiency of the pump or changing the pipe system to incorporate more efficient fittings or smoother pipes, such that total head loss is reduced.
Nutrient Procurement. From Table 1, there is a dramatic differ- ence in energy consumption for nutrients (mainly CO2 because N

and P come from manure waste) between the OPS and ATS sys- tems. In the OPS, pure CO2 or air is generally bubbled into the growth medium to provide a carbon source for algae photosynthe- sis (Benemann and Oswald, 1996). In this study, upstream burdens associated with the production of CO2 account for nearly one-fifth (17%) of total energy usage in the OPS system. This is consistent with previous OPS studies (Clarens et al., 2010, Lundquist et al., 2010). In contrast, the shallow, continuously pumped ATS configu- ration requires neither commercial fertilizer nor CO2 beyond what can be taken up from the atmosphere. This is one of the principal benefits of the ATS for on-farm dairy waste nutrient management (Mulbry et al., 2008a).

Upstream Infrastructure Burdens. Table 1 indicates that there is a dramatic difference in the extent to which upstream energy use for production of infrastructure contributes to overall energy con- sumption in each algae system. Annualized infrastructure energy burdens account for 50% of total energy use per FU in the OPS sys- tem but only 10% in the ATS. This difference makes re-design of the OPS infrastructure a potentially useful strategy for improving over- all energy efficiency of algae-to-energy systems. In contrast, it would seem not worthwhile to try to optimize ATS infrastructure burdens because they account for such a small fraction of overall energy consumption in that system.

In summary, referring again to Fig. 2 and Table 1, the AD sce- nario is most energetically favorable because it mediates the larg- est difference between energy outputs (both direct and indirect) and energy inputs (direct, upstream, and infrastructure). In com- parison, the OPS and ATS scenarios exhibit increased energy pro- duction, but the increases in energy output do not counterbalance the increased energy consumption required for construction and operation of either algae scenario. The REF sce- nario is least energetically desirable, even though it consumes the least amount of energy per FU, because it produces such a small amount of energy offset.

From Table 1, OPS and ATS exhibit small difference in overall energy consumption (input) per FU, but allocation of energy use is very different between the two. The OPS system exhibits consid- erable balance among three principal energy-consuming entities: algae cultivation consumes 22% of the total, operation of the anaer- obic digester consumes 28%, and annualized upstream infrastruc- ture burdens accounts for 50%. In contrast, the ATS system is less balanced, with greater energy consumption for algae cultivation (62%), less consumption for operation of the anaerobic digester (28%), and much smaller annualized upstream infrastructure bur- dens (10%). Two especially interesting implications of energy use allocation include: (1) the balance between direct and indirect use; and (2) the extent to which improvement in selected sub- processes could improve overall energy bottom line. These are discussed in the following paragraphs.

Regarding direct versus upstream energy consumption, an algae farming entity is likely most interested in minimizing direct, on- farm electricity and heat consumption (e.g., for pumping, diges- tion, etc.), since these have an economic cost. In contrast, there is currently no mandatory mechanism for monetizing upstream en- ergy consumption (e.g., for production of virgin CO2 or manufac- ture of infrastructure materials). In Table 1, direct energy use for algae cultivation and digestion processes constitutes 50% of total energy consumption per FU in OPS and 90% in ATS. This may make OPS seemingly more desirable than ATS for an algae farming entity, together with the fact that the total amount of energy consumed per FU is also smaller in OPS than in ATS.

With respect to possible system improvements for each nutrient management scenario, it is desirable to focus design efforts on a small number of particularly energy intensive sub-processes. Thus, systems exhibiting "unbalanced" energy consumption (whereby a small number of sub-processes constitute the majority of overall energy consumption) may be most ripe for overall improvement. From Table 1, both OPS and ATS exhibit several key sub-processes, which, if improved, could register an appreciable reduction on overall energy consumption per FU. But ATS exhibits less balanced energy consumption compared to OPS. As such, a 50% improve- ment in ATS electricity usage alone (596 GJ) would mediate greater energy savings than a 25% improvement in energy intensiveness in OPS algae cultivation + digestion + OPS infrastructure (68 + 85 + 157 MJ = 310 Of course, not all sub-processes are equally likely to be improved. Of the items listed in Table 1, topics of interest for near-term improvement via decreased energy con- sumption include: electricity consumption, nutrient supply, and infrastructure burdens. These are discussed in the following paragraphs.

Electricity Consumption. Direct energy use will be a key factor in decision making about on-farm waste nutrient management, be- cause farms must pay for both heat and electricity. From Table 1, heat consumption is roughly the same in both algae systems (~30 GJ/FU), but electricity consumption varies dramatically: 363 GJ/FU for OPS versus 1,193 GJ/FU for ATS. Additional electricity consumption in ATS is not offset by additional electricity produc- tion, since both algae systems produce roughly the same amount of bioelectricity (1,700 GJ/FU). The difference in electricity con- sumption between OPS and ATS arises mostly from cultivation usage (55 GJ/FU in OPS versus 846 GJ/FU in ATS), since both sys- tems use about the same amount for anaerobic digestion. Cultiva- tion-phase electricity usage of ATS is primarily for pumping water up out of the sump to the head of the floway, which accounts for 47% of overall energy usage. Two ways to reduce pump electricity consumption include: increasing the efficiency of the pump or changing the pipe system to incorporate more efficient fittings or smoother pipes, such that total head loss is reduced.
Nutrient Procurement. From Table 1, there is a dramatic differ- ence in energy consumption for nutrients (mainly CO2 because N

and P come from manure waste) between the OPS and ATS sys- tems. In the OPS, pure CO2 or air is generally bubbled into the growth medium to provide a carbon source for algae photosynthe- sis (Benemann and Oswald, 1996). In this study, upstream burdens associated with the production of CO2 account for nearly one-fifth (17%) of total energy usage in the OPS system. This is consistent with previous OPS studies (Clarens et al., 2010, Lundquist et al., 2010). In contrast, the shallow, continuously pumped ATS configu- ration requires neither commercial fertilizer nor CO2 beyond what can be taken up from the atmosphere. This is one of the principal benefits of the ATS for on-farm dairy waste nutrient management (Mulbry et al., 2008a).

Upstream Infrastructure Burdens. Table 1 indicates that there is a dramatic difference in the extent to which upstream energy use for production of infrastructure contributes to overall energy con- sumption in each algae system. Annualized infrastructure energy burdens account for 50% of total energy use per FU in the OPS sys- tem but only 10% in the ATS. This difference makes re-design of the OPS infrastructure a potentially useful strategy for improving over- all energy efficiency of algae-to-energy systems. In contrast, it would seem not worthwhile to try to optimize ATS infrastructure burdens because they account for such a small fraction of overall energy consumption in that system.

In summary, referring again to Fig. 2 and Table 1, the AD sce- nario is most energetically favorable because it mediates the larg- est difference between energy outputs (both direct and indirect) and energy inputs (direct, upstream, and infrastructure). In com- parison, the OPS and ATS scenarios exhibit increased energy pro- duction, but the increases in energy output do not counterbalance the increased energy consumption required for construction and operation of either algae scenario. The REF sce- nario is least energetically desirable, even though it consumes the least amount of energy per FU, because it produces such a small amount of energy offset.
13: <header>3.3.2. Net energy use (EN) </header>

It was expected that enhanced EUT performance may be the pri- mary reason for farms to consider alternatives to the REF manure waste management strategy. However, it was also hypothesized that use of AD, OPS, or ATS in place of REF could deliver additional environmental benefits. In particular, it was expected that addition of an energy-producing process could transform manure waste management from an economic and environmental liability into a means of generating additional farm revenue. In this vein, anaer- obic digestion seemed an obvious choice, since there is already some precedent for on-farm dairy waste digestion (Van Horn et al., 1994). Beyond this, it was hypothesized that addition of an algae cultivation system could simultaneously increase the amount of digestible biomass produced on-farm and improve nutrient management (Section 3.2.1). It has been previously dem- onstrated that algae exhibiting lipid contents less than 40% (including many native OPS and ATS crops) are unsuitable for bio- diesel production and are thus better leveraged via anaerobic digestion (Sialve et al., 2009). Thus, there was significant motiva- tion for exploration of on-farm manure waste management sys- tems coupling anaerobic digestion and algae cultivation, but it was unknown how energetically favorable these systems would be and how they would compare to each other.

From Fig. 2, AD, OPS, and ATS exhibit net energy production per FU. Only REF exhibits net energy consumption. From this, anaero- bic digestion must be an important differentiating factor, presum- ably because it mediates two types of energy production: direct energy in the form of bioelectricity, and indirect energy as solid dig- estate residuals, which can supplant commercial fertilizers. Regarding direct energy generation, for AD, only the manure slurry is digested to produce biogas. For OPS and ATS, manure slurry is co-digested with algae biomass. For a 100-cow farm, the amounts of volatile material digested in each system are 209,150 kg/yr for AD, 311,467 kg/yr for OPS, and 338,508 kg/yr for ATS. For OPS and ATS, algae biomass constitutes roughly 40% of total digestible biomass. The increased amount of volatile material for OPS and ATS would necessitate significant increases in digester capacity to maintain the same retention time as in AD. These increases (70-100%) were accounted for as increased materials usage in OPS and ATS. The amounts of CH4-derived bioelectricity produced per FU are 1,044 GJ/yr for AD, 1,662 GJ/yr for OPS, and 1,802 GJ/yr

for ATS, assuming digestion yields are 0.42 L CH4/g DS manure (ash free) and 0.49 L CH4/g DS algae (ash free). The algae methane yield could be an underestimate, since there is bench-scale evidence to suggest that this quantity increases in the presence of a carbon- rich digestion co-substrate (e.g., manure) (Yen and Brune, 2007); however, this possible impact could not be accounted for due to data scarcity.

Regarding indirect energy generation, the masses of solid dige- state produced per FU are 156, 232, and 256 Mg/yr in the AD, OPS, and ATS scenarios, respectively. Multiplying these by a bioavail- ability equivalency factor and the amount of energy required to produce commercial fertilizer (37.5 MJ/kg H12N3O4P and 62 MJ/kg urea) (Weidema, 2007), the energy offsets embodied by production of soil amendment are: 101 GJ/yr for AD, 189 GJ/yr for OPS, and 199 GJ/yr for ATS. REF produces only a small amount of energy pro- duction, this as an offset associated with production of corn silage:
1.1 GJ/ha per FU, assuming 0.36 MJ/kg corn.

Energy consumption was also evaluated for each nutrient man- agement strategy. Not surprisingly, REF requires the lowest energy consumption (199 GJ/yr per FU) because it involves the least num- ber of steps. Energy consumption in this scenario is mostly associ- ated with operation of manure spreading equipment, including upstream impacts associated with production of the necessary die- sel fuel.

Energy consumption for AD (301 GJ/yr per FU) was roughly 1.5 times that of REF. This is because land application of liquid dige- state consumes roughly half (61%) of the total energy required for the AD scenario, and this quantity is held in common between the AD and REF scenarios. The other main energy consuming pro- cess for AD is operation of the digester, which comprises 33% of overall AD energy consumption. Interestingly, annualized energy consumption for "upstream" production of AD infrastructure is nearly negligible, accounting for only 6% of annual AD energy con- sumption when divided by the digester's assumed useful life years).

Energy consumption for OPS (1,240 GJ/yr) and ATS (1,375 GJ/yr) was understandably greater than for either AD or REF. This is be- cause additional energy is required beyond what is consumed for operation of the anaerobic digester and application of the liquid digestate. The larger number of processes and material inputs re- quired for both algae cultivation scenarios makes it worthwhile to investigate the allocation of overall energy consumption in each scenario, as means to identify possible improvements.

for ATS, assuming digestion yields are 0.42 L CH4/g DS manure (ash free) and 0.49 L CH4/g DS algae (ash free). Regarding indirect energy generation, the masses of solid dige- state produced per FU are 156, 232, and 256 Mg/yr in the AD, OPS, and ATS scenarios, respectively. Multiplying these by a bioavail- ability equivalency factor and the amount of energy required to produce commercial fertilizer (37.5 MJ/kg H12N3O4P and 62 MJ/kg urea) (Weidema, 2007), the energy offsets embodied by production of soil amendment are: 101 GJ/yr for AD, 189 GJ/yr for OPS, and 199 GJ/yr for ATS. REF produces only a small amount of energy pro- duction, this as an offset associated with production of corn silage:
1.1 GJ/ha per FU, assuming 0.36 MJ/kg corn.

Energy consumption was also evaluated for each nutrient man- agement strategy. Not surprisingly, REF requires the lowest energy consumption (199 GJ/yr per FU) because it involves the least num- ber of steps. Energy consumption in this scenario is mostly associ- ated with operation of manure spreading equipment, including upstream impacts associated with production of the necessary die- sel fuel.

Energy consumption for AD (301 GJ/yr per FU) was roughly 1.5 times that of REF. This is because land application of liquid dige- state consumes roughly half (61%) of the total energy required for the AD scenario, and this quantity is held in common between the AD and REF scenarios. The other main energy consuming pro- cess for AD is operation of the digester, which comprises 33% of overall AD energy consumption. Interestingly, annualized energy consumption for "upstream" production of AD infrastructure is nearly negligible, accounting for only 6% of annual AD energy con- sumption when divided by the digester's assumed useful life years).

Energy consumption for OPS (1,240 GJ/yr) and ATS (1,375 GJ/yr) was understandably greater than for either AD or REF. This is be- cause additional energy is required beyond what is consumed for operation of the anaerobic digester and application of the liquid digestate. The larger number of processes and material inputs re- quired for both algae cultivation scenarios makes it worthwhile to investigate the allocation of overall energy consumption in each scenario, as means to identify possible improvements.

From Table 1, OPS and ATS exhibit small difference in overall energy consumption (input) per FU, but allocation of energy use is very different between the two. The OPS system exhibits consid- erable balance among three principal energy-consuming entities: algae cultivation consumes 22% of the total, operation of the anaer- obic digester consumes 28%, and annualized upstream infrastruc- ture burdens accounts for 50%. In contrast, the ATS system is less balanced, with greater energy consumption for algae cultivation (62%), less consumption for operation of the anaerobic digester (28%), and much smaller annualized upstream infrastructure bur- dens (10%). Two especially interesting implications of energy use allocation include: (1) the balance between direct and indirect use; and (2) the extent to which improvement in selected sub- processes could improve overall energy bottom line. These are discussed in the following paragraphs.

Regarding direct versus upstream energy consumption, an algae farming entity is likely most interested in minimizing direct, on- farm electricity and heat consumption (e.g., for pumping, diges- tion, etc.), since these have an economic cost. In contrast, there is currently no mandatory mechanism for monetizing upstream en- ergy consumption (e.g., for production of virgin CO2 or manufac- ture of infrastructure materials). In Table 1, direct energy use for algae cultivation and digestion processes constitutes 50% of total energy consumption per FU in OPS and 90% in ATS. This may make OPS seemingly more desirable than ATS for an algae farming entity, together with the fact that the total amount of energy consumed per FU is also smaller in OPS than in ATS.

With respect to possible system improvements for each nutrient management scenario, it is desirable to focus design efforts on a small number of particularly energy intensive sub-processes. Thus, systems exhibiting "unbalanced" energy consumption (whereby a small number of sub-processes constitute the majority of overall energy consumption) may be most ripe for overall improvement. From Table 1, both OPS and ATS exhibit several key sub-processes, which, if improved, could register an appreciable reduction on overall energy consumption per FU. But ATS exhibits less balanced energy consumption compared to OPS. As such, a 50% improve- ment in ATS electricity usage alone (596 GJ) would mediate greater energy savings than a 25% improvement in energy intensiveness in OPS algae cultivation + digestion + OPS infrastructure (68 + 85 + 157 MJ = 310 Of course, not all sub-processes are equally likely to be improved. Of the items listed in Table 1, topics of interest for near-term improvement via decreased energy con- sumption include: electricity consumption, nutrient supply, and infrastructure burdens. These are discussed in the following paragraphs.

Electricity Consumption. Direct energy use will be a key factor in decision making about on-farm waste nutrient management, be- cause farms must pay for both heat and electricity. From Table 1, heat consumption is roughly the same in both algae systems (~30 GJ/FU), but electricity consumption varies dramatically: 363 GJ/FU for OPS versus 1,193 GJ/FU for ATS. Additional electricity consumption in ATS is not offset by additional electricity produc- tion, since both algae systems produce roughly the same amount of bioelectricity (1,700 GJ/FU). The difference in electricity con- sumption between OPS and ATS arises mostly from cultivation usage (55 GJ/FU in OPS versus 846 GJ/FU in ATS), since both sys- tems use about the same amount for anaerobic digestion. Cultiva- tion-phase electricity usage of ATS is primarily for pumping water up out of the sump to the head of the floway, which accounts for 47% of overall energy usage. Two ways to reduce pump electricity consumption include: increasing the efficiency of the pump or changing the pipe system to incorporate more efficient fittings or smoother pipes, such that total head loss is reduced.
Nutrient Procurement. From Table 1, there is a dramatic differ- ence in energy consumption for nutrients (mainly CO2 because N

and P come from manure waste) between the OPS and ATS sys- tems. In the OPS, pure CO2 or air is generally bubbled into the growth medium to provide a carbon source for algae photosynthe- sis (Benemann and Oswald, 1996). In this study, upstream burdens associated with the production of CO2 account for nearly one-fifth (17%) of total energy usage in the OPS system. This is consistent with previous OPS studies (Clarens et al., 2010, Lundquist et al., 2010). In contrast, the shallow, continuously pumped ATS configu- ration requires neither commercial fertilizer nor CO2 beyond what can be taken up from the atmosphere. This is one of the principal benefits of the ATS for on-farm dairy waste nutrient management (Mulbry et al., 2008a).

Upstream Infrastructure Burdens. Table 1 indicates that there is a dramatic difference in the extent to which upstream energy use for production of infrastructure contributes to overall energy con- sumption in each algae system. Annualized infrastructure energy burdens account for 50% of total energy use per FU in the OPS sys- tem but only 10% in the ATS. This difference makes re-design of the OPS infrastructure a potentially useful strategy for improving over- all energy efficiency of algae-to-energy systems. In contrast, it would seem not worthwhile to try to optimize ATS infrastructure burdens because they account for such a small fraction of overall energy consumption in that system.

In summary, referring again to Fig. 2 and Table 1, the AD sce- nario is most energetically favorable because it mediates the larg- est difference between energy outputs (both direct and indirect) and energy inputs (direct, upstream, and infrastructure). In com- parison, the OPS and ATS scenarios exhibit increased energy pro- duction, but the increases in energy output do not counterbalance the increased energy consumption required for construction and operation of either algae scenario. The REF sce- nario is least energetically desirable, even though it consumes the least amount of energy per FU, because it produces such a small amount of energy offset.

18: <header>3.3.2. Net energy use (EN) </header>

It was expected that enhanced EUT performance may be the pri- mary reason for farms to consider alternatives to the REF manure waste management strategy. However, it was also hypothesized that use of AD, OPS, or ATS in place of REF could deliver additional environmental benefits. In particular, it was expected that addition of an energy-producing process could transform manure waste management from an economic and environmental liability into a means of generating additional farm revenue. In this vein, anaer- obic digestion seemed an obvious choice, since there is already some precedent for on-farm dairy waste digestion (Van Horn et al., 1994). Beyond this, it was hypothesized that addition of an algae cultivation system could simultaneously increase the amount of digestible biomass produced on-farm and improve nutrient management (Section 3.2.1). It has been previously dem- onstrated that algae exhibiting lipid contents less than 40% (including many native OPS and ATS crops) are unsuitable for bio- diesel production and are thus better leveraged via anaerobic digestion (Sialve et al., 2009). Thus, there was significant motiva- tion for exploration of on-farm manure waste management sys- tems coupling anaerobic digestion and algae cultivation, but it was unknown how energetically favorable these systems would be and how they would compare to each other.

From Fig. 2, AD, OPS, and ATS exhibit net energy production per FU. Only REF exhibits net energy consumption. From this, anaero- bic digestion must be an important differentiating factor, presum- ably because it mediates two types of energy production: direct energy in the form of bioelectricity, and indirect energy as solid dig- estate residuals, which can supplant commercial fertilizers. Regarding direct energy generation, for AD, only the manure slurry is digested to produce biogas. For OPS and ATS, manure slurry is co-digested with algae biomass. For a 100-cow farm, the amounts of volatile material digested in each system are 209,150 kg/yr for AD, 311,467 kg/yr for OPS, and 338,508 kg/yr for ATS. For OPS and ATS, algae biomass constitutes roughly 40% of total digestible biomass. The increased amount of volatile material for OPS and ATS would necessitate significant increases in digester capacity to maintain the same retention time as in AD. These increases (70-100%) were accounted for as increased materials usage in OPS and ATS. The amounts of CH4-derived bioelectricity produced per FU are 1,044 GJ/yr for AD, 1,662 GJ/yr for OPS, and 1,802 GJ/yr

for ATS, assuming digestion yields are 0.42 L CH4/g DS manure (ash free) and 0.49 L CH4/g DS algae (ash free). The algae methane yield could be an underestimate, since there is bench-scale evidence to suggest that this quantity increases in the presence of a carbon- rich digestion co-substrate (e.g., manure) (Yen and Brune, 2007); however, this possible impact could not be accounted for due to data scarcity.

Regarding indirect energy generation, the masses of solid dige- state produced per FU are 156, 232, and 256 Mg/yr in the AD, OPS, and ATS scenarios, respectively. Multiplying these by a bioavail- ability equivalency factor and the amount of energy required to produce commercial fertilizer (37.5 MJ/kg H12N3O4P and 62 MJ/kg urea) (Weidema, 2007), the energy offsets embodied by production of soil amendment are: 101 GJ/yr for AD, 189 GJ/yr for OPS, and 199 GJ/yr for ATS. REF produces only a small amount of energy pro- duction, this as an offset associated with production of corn silage:
1.1 GJ/ha per FU, assuming 0.36 MJ/kg corn.

Energy consumption was also evaluated for each nutrient man- agement strategy. Not surprisingly, REF requires the lowest energy consumption (199 GJ/yr per FU) because it involves the least num- ber of steps. Energy consumption in this scenario is mostly associ- ated with operation of manure spreading equipment, including upstream impacts associated with production of the necessary die- sel fuel.

Energy consumption for AD (301 GJ/yr per FU) was roughly 1.5 times that of REF. This is because land application of liquid dige- state consumes roughly half (61%) of the total energy required for the AD scenario, and this quantity is held in common between the AD and REF scenarios. The other main energy consuming pro- cess for AD is operation of the digester, which comprises 33% of overall AD energy consumption. Interestingly, annualized energy consumption for "upstream" production of AD infrastructure is nearly negligible, accounting for only 6% of annual AD energy con- sumption when divided by the digester's assumed useful life years).

Energy consumption for OPS (1,240 GJ/yr) and ATS (1,375 GJ/yr) was understandably greater than for either AD or REF. This is be- cause additional energy is required beyond what is consumed for operation of the anaerobic digester and application of the liquid digestate. The larger number of processes and material inputs re- quired for both algae cultivation scenarios makes it worthwhile to investigate the allocation of overall energy consumption in each scenario, as means to identify possible improvements.

From Table 1, OPS and ATS exhibit small difference in overall energy consumption (input) per FU, but allocation of energy use is very different between the two. The OPS system exhibits consid- erable balance among three principal energy-consuming entities: algae cultivation consumes 22% of the total, operation of the anaer- obic digester consumes 28%, and annualized upstream infrastruc- ture burdens accounts for 50%. In contrast, the ATS system is less balanced, with greater energy consumption for algae cultivation (62%), less consumption for operation of the anaerobic digester (28%), and much smaller annualized upstream infrastructure bur- dens (10%). Two especially interesting implications of energy use allocation include: (1) the balance between direct and indirect use; and (2) the extent to which improvement in selected sub- processes could improve overall energy bottom line. These are discussed in the following paragraphs.

Regarding direct versus upstream energy consumption, an algae farming entity is likely most interested in minimizing direct, on- farm electricity and heat consumption (e.g., for pumping, diges- tion, etc.), since these have an economic cost. In contrast, there is currently no mandatory mechanism for monetizing upstream en- ergy consumption (e.g., for production of virgin CO2 or manufac- ture of infrastructure materials). In Table 1, direct energy use for algae cultivation and digestion processes constitutes 50% of total energy consumption per FU in OPS and 90% in ATS. This may make OPS seemingly more desirable than ATS for an algae farming entity, together with the fact that the total amount of energy consumed per FU is also smaller in OPS than in ATS.

With respect to possible system improvements for each nutrient management scenario, it is desirable to focus design efforts on a small number of particularly energy intensive sub-processes. Thus, systems exhibiting "unbalanced" energy consumption (whereby a small number of sub-processes constitute the majority of overall energy consumption) may be most ripe for overall improvement. From Table 1, both OPS and ATS exhibit several key sub-processes, which, if improved, could register an appreciable reduction on overall energy consumption per FU. But ATS exhibits less balanced energy consumption compared to OPS. As such, a 50% improve- ment in ATS electricity usage alone (596 GJ) would mediate greater energy savings than a 25% improvement in energy intensiveness in OPS algae cultivation + digestion + OPS infrastructure (68 + 85 + 157 MJ = 310 Of course, not all sub-processes are equally likely to be improved. Of the items listed in Table 1, topics of interest for near-term improvement via decreased energy con- sumption include: electricity consumption, nutrient supply, and infrastructure burdens. These are discussed in the following paragraphs.

Electricity Consumption. Direct energy use will be a key factor in decision making about on-farm waste nutrient management, be- cause farms must pay for both heat and electricity. From Table 1, heat consumption is roughly the same in both algae systems (~30 GJ/FU), but electricity consumption varies dramatically: 363 GJ/FU for OPS versus 1,193 GJ/FU for ATS. Additional electricity consumption in ATS is not offset by additional electricity produc- tion, since both algae systems produce roughly the same amount of bioelectricity (1,700 GJ/FU). The difference in electricity con- sumption between OPS and ATS arises mostly from cultivation usage (55 GJ/FU in OPS versus 846 GJ/FU in ATS), since both sys- tems use about the same amount for anaerobic digestion. Cultiva- tion-phase electricity usage of ATS is primarily for pumping water up out of the sump to the head of the floway, which accounts for 47% of overall energy usage. Two ways to reduce pump electricity consumption include: increasing the efficiency of the pump or changing the pipe system to incorporate more efficient fittings or smoother pipes, such that total head loss is reduced.
Nutrient Procurement. From Table 1, there is a dramatic differ- ence in energy consumption for nutrients (mainly CO2 because N

and P come from manure waste) between the OPS and ATS sys- tems. In the OPS, pure CO2 or air is generally bubbled into the growth medium to provide a carbon source for algae photosynthe- sis (Benemann and Oswald, 1996). In this study, upstream burdens associated with the production of CO2 account for nearly one-fifth (17%) of total energy usage in the OPS system. This is consistent with previous OPS studies (Clarens et al., 2010, Lundquist et al., 2010). In contrast, the shallow, continuously pumped ATS configu- ration requires neither commercial fertilizer nor CO2 beyond what can be taken up from the atmosphere. This is one of the principal benefits of the ATS for on-farm dairy waste nutrient management (Mulbry et al., 2008a).

Upstream Infrastructure Burdens. Table 1 indicates that there is a dramatic difference in the extent to which upstream energy use for production of infrastructure contributes to overall energy con- sumption in each algae system. Annualized infrastructure energy burdens account for 50% of total energy use per FU in the OPS sys- tem but only 10% in the ATS. This difference makes re-design of the OPS infrastructure a potentially useful strategy for improving over- all energy efficiency of algae-to-energy systems. In contrast, it would seem not worthwhile to try to optimize ATS infrastructure burdens because they account for such a small fraction of overall energy consumption in that system.

In summary, referring again to Fig. 2 and Table 1, the AD sce- nario is most energetically favorable because it mediates the larg- est difference between energy outputs (both direct and indirect) and energy inputs (direct, upstream, and infrastructure). In com- parison, the OPS and ATS scenarios exhibit increased energy pro- duction, but the increases in energy output do not counterbalance the increased energy consumption required for construction and operation of either algae scenario. The REF sce- nario is least energetically desirable, even though it consumes the least amount of energy per FU, because it produces such a small amount of energy offset.

Electricity Consumption. Direct energy use will be a key factor in decision making about on-farm waste nutrient management, be- cause farms must pay for both heat and electricity. From Table 1, heat consumption is roughly the same in both algae systems (~30 GJ/FU), but electricity consumption varies dramatically: 363 GJ/FU for OPS versus 1,193 GJ/FU for ATS. Additional electricity consumption in ATS is not offset by additional electricity produc- tion, since both algae systems produce roughly the same amount of bioelectricity (1,700 GJ/FU). The difference in electricity con- sumption between OPS and ATS arises mostly from cultivation usage (55 GJ/FU in OPS versus 846 GJ/FU in ATS), since both sys- tems use about the same amount for anaerobic digestion. Cultiva- tion-phase electricity usage of ATS is primarily for pumping water up out of the sump to the head of the floway, which accounts for 47% of overall energy usage. Two ways to reduce pump electricity consumption include: increasing the efficiency of the pump or changing the pipe system to incorporate more efficient fittings or smoother pipes, such that total head loss is reduced.
Nutrient Procurement. From Table 1, there is a dramatic differ- ence in energy consumption for nutrients (mainly CO2 because N

and P come from manure waste) between the OPS and ATS sys- tems. In the OPS, pure CO2 or air is generally bubbled into the growth medium to provide a carbon source for algae photosynthe- sis (Benemann and Oswald, 1996). In this study, upstream burdens associated with the production of CO2 account for nearly one-fifth (17%) of total energy usage in the OPS system. This is consistent with previous OPS studies (Clarens et al., 2010, Lundquist et al., 2010). In contrast, the shallow, continuously pumped ATS configu- ration requires neither commercial fertilizer nor CO2 beyond what can be taken up from the atmosphere. This is one of the principal benefits of the ATS for on-farm dairy waste nutrient management (Mulbry et al., 2008a).

Upstream Infrastructure Burdens. Table 1 indicates that there is a dramatic difference in the extent to which upstream energy use for production of infrastructure contributes to overall energy con- sumption in each algae system. Annualized infrastructure energy burdens account for 50% of total energy use per FU in the OPS sys- tem but only 10% in the ATS. This difference makes re-design of the OPS infrastructure a potentially useful strategy for improving over- all energy efficiency of algae-to-energy systems. In contrast, it would seem not worthwhile to try to optimize ATS infrastructure burdens because they account for such a small fraction of overall energy consumption in that system.

In summary, referring again to Fig. 2 and Table 1, the AD sce- nario is most energetically favorable because it mediates the larg- est difference between energy outputs (both direct and indirect) and energy inputs (direct, upstream, and infrastructure). In com- parison, the OPS and ATS scenarios exhibit increased energy pro- duction, but the increases in energy output do not counterbalance the increased energy consumption required for construction and operation of either algae scenario. The REF sce- nario is least energetically desirable, even though it consumes the least amount of energy per FU, because it produces such a small amount of energy offset.
21: <header>3.3.2. Net energy use (EN) </header>

It was expected that enhanced EUT performance may be the pri- mary reason for farms to consider alternatives to the REF manure waste management strategy. However, it was also hypothesized that use of AD, OPS, or ATS in place of REF could deliver additional environmental benefits. In particular, it was expected that addition of an energy-producing process could transform manure waste management from an economic and environmental liability into a means of generating additional farm revenue. In this vein, anaer- obic digestion seemed an obvious choice, since there is already some precedent for on-farm dairy waste digestion (Van Horn et al., 1994). Beyond this, it was hypothesized that addition of an algae cultivation system could simultaneously increase the amount of digestible biomass produced on-farm and improve nutrient management (Section 3.2.1). It has been previously dem- onstrated that algae exhibiting lipid contents less than 40% (including many native OPS and ATS crops) are unsuitable for bio- diesel production and are thus better leveraged via anaerobic digestion (Sialve et al., 2009). Thus, there was significant motiva- tion for exploration of on-farm manure waste management sys- tems coupling anaerobic digestion and algae cultivation, but it was unknown how energetically favorable these systems would be and how they would compare to each other.

From Fig. 2, AD, OPS, and ATS exhibit net energy production per FU. Only REF exhibits net energy consumption. From this, anaero- bic digestion must be an important differentiating factor, presum- ably because it mediates two types of energy production: direct energy in the form of bioelectricity, and indirect energy as solid dig- estate residuals, which can supplant commercial fertilizers. Regarding direct energy generation, for AD, only the manure slurry is digested to produce biogas. For OPS and ATS, manure slurry is co-digested with algae biomass. For a 100-cow farm, the amounts of volatile material digested in each system are 209,150 kg/yr for AD, 311,467 kg/yr for OPS, and 338,508 kg/yr for ATS. For OPS and ATS, algae biomass constitutes roughly 40% of total digestible biomass. The increased amount of volatile material for OPS and ATS would necessitate significant increases in digester capacity to maintain the same retention time as in AD. These increases (70-100%) were accounted for as increased materials usage in OPS and ATS. The amounts of CH4-derived bioelectricity produced per FU are 1,044 GJ/yr for AD, 1,662 GJ/yr for OPS, and 1,802 GJ/yr

for ATS, assuming digestion yields are 0.42 L CH4/g DS manure (ash free) and 0.49 L CH4/g DS algae (ash free). The algae methane yield could be an underestimate, since there is bench-scale evidence to suggest that this quantity increases in the presence of a carbon- rich digestion co-substrate (e.g., manure) (Yen and Brune, 2007); however, this possible impact could not be accounted for due to data scarcity.

Regarding indirect energy generation, the masses of solid dige- state produced per FU are 156, 232, and 256 Mg/yr in the AD, OPS, and ATS scenarios, respectively. Multiplying these by a bioavail- ability equivalency factor and the amount of energy required to produce commercial fertilizer (37.5 MJ/kg H12N3O4P and 62 MJ/kg urea) (Weidema, 2007), the energy offsets embodied by production of soil amendment are: 101 GJ/yr for AD, 189 GJ/yr for OPS, and 199 GJ/yr for ATS. REF produces only a small amount of energy pro- duction, this as an offset associated with production of corn silage:
1.1 GJ/ha per FU, assuming 0.36 MJ/kg corn.

Energy consumption was also evaluated for each nutrient man- agement strategy. Not surprisingly, REF requires the lowest energy consumption (199 GJ/yr per FU) because it involves the least num- ber of steps. Energy consumption in this scenario is mostly associ- ated with operation of manure spreading equipment, including upstream impacts associated with production of the necessary die- sel fuel.

Energy consumption for AD (301 GJ/yr per FU) was roughly 1.5 times that of REF. This is because land application of liquid dige- state consumes roughly half (61%) of the total energy required for the AD scenario, and this quantity is held in common between the AD and REF scenarios. The other main energy consuming pro- cess for AD is operation of the digester, which comprises 33% of overall AD energy consumption. Interestingly, annualized energy consumption for "upstream" production of AD infrastructure is nearly negligible, accounting for only 6% of annual AD energy con- sumption when divided by the digester's assumed useful life years).

Energy consumption for OPS (1,240 GJ/yr) and ATS (1,375 GJ/yr) was understandably greater than for either AD or REF. This is be- cause additional energy is required beyond what is consumed for operation of the anaerobic digester and application of the liquid digestate. The larger number of processes and material inputs re- quired for both algae cultivation scenarios makes it worthwhile to investigate the allocation of overall energy consumption in each scenario, as means to identify possible improvements.

23: There is frequently a link between energy consumption and greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions per FU, thus it is not surprising that the EN and GWP performances exhibit qualitatively similar trends in Fig. 2. For both impacts, the REF scenario exhibits the worst performance (437 Mg CO2-eq/FU). This is attributed to the significant emissions of gaseous CH4 and N20, both of which have very strong GWP impacts.

The GWP performances of AD (337 CO2-eq/FU), OPS (309 Mg CO2-eq/FU), and ATS (242 Mg CO2-eq/FU) are all better than REF, for at least two reasons. First, all three improved systems dramatically reduce emissions of strongly global-warming CH4 and N20. Second, all three include one or more CO2-sequestering pro- cesses; e.g., anaerobic digestion and/or CO2 recycling. During digestion, not all organic material is converted into biogas. Thus, 80% of the carbon fraction in the residual solid "digestate", roughly 30% (Hue and Sobieszczyk, 1999; Moorhead et al., 1987; Tambone et al., 2009), is accounted for as GWP sequestration, because it is not directly returned to the atmosphere when the biogas is com- busted. With respect to CO2 recycling, only the ATS system exhibits true CO2 reuse, whereby the flux of CO2 out of the atmosphere into algae biomass (via photosynthesis) is roughly equivalent to the flux of CO2 into the atmosphere during biogas combustion. Thus, ATS is nearly net CO2-neutral for direct GHG flows. In contrast, OPS requires large quantities of compressed virgin CO2 as input for algae cultivation, such that the combustion GHG flux cannot be directly offset by photosynthesis. Moreover, the manufacture of the virgin CO2 engenders significant upstream GWP emissions. Thus, the combined direct and upstream GWP impacts associated with commercial CO2 render OPS worse than AD for GWP performance.

25: <header>3.4. Economic feasibility </header>

Environmental impacts, as articulated using life cycle assess- ment (LCA), are only one component of sustainability decision- making when it comes to on-farm management of dairy manure waste. Farmers must also consider the relative economic favorabil- ity of all possible manure management strategies. Thus, a life cycle costing (LCC) analysis was undertaken in parallel with the LCA.

In estimating economic feasibility of the various manure waste management scenarios, two types of expenditures were consid- ered: capital costs and annual operational costs. Capital costs ac- counted for initial investments for infrastructure, major facilities, working capital, engineering, and contingencies. Land costs were excluded, because direct land use in all four systems is much smal- ler than the typical farm size in this region (Section 3.2), and it was assumed that sufficient land could be allocated for waste manage- ment from the existing farm area. Annual operational costs ac- counted for energy and materials (electricity, fuel, fertilizer, etc.), labor, capital charges, insurance, and systems maintenance. Addi- tionally, five possible revenue sources were evaluated: (1) sale of bioelectricity back to the grid at $0.07/KW-hr; (2) sale of nutrient credits at $20/lb-N; (3) sale of carbon credits at $0.005/kg CO2; (4) sale of solid digestate as soil amendment at $7.8-15.6/Mg dig- estate; and (5) sale of corn at $234/Mg. (See Supplementary SI for full revenue distributions.) It was assumed that REF could only ac- crue corn revenues, by selling the crop that is produced on the lands where dairy waste is applied as soil amendment. Conversely, OPS and ATS could achieve all types of revenues except sale of corn, because there is no need for land application of either raw or di- gested manure waste. Thus, only AD was expected to achieve all five possible revenue streams, since digestions yield not only bio- electricity, N/CO2 credits, and solid digestate, but also nutrient-rich liquid digestate which is land-applied on fields for corn cultivation.

Fig. 3a summarizes the results of the economic analysis, show- ing net present value (NPV) over an assumed 20-years project life for all waste management strategies (in Panel a). Thus, it is valuable to assess the applicability of existing and emerging nutrient credit market frameworks. Such markets currently exist in Maryland, Pennsylvania, Virginia, and West Vir- ginia, all of which contain watersheds draining to the Chesapeake Bay. Although some elements of the trading programs in each state are very similar, the definition and specific requirements for credit trading do exhibit some differences, including: what pollutants can be traded, permissible trading ratios, eligibility requirements for credit sellers, etc. (Branosky et al., 2011). Per the Pennsylvania nutrient trading framework, a 20% reduction compared to baseline is defined as the threshold for nutrient credit generation (Branosky et al., 2011). As such, a farm utilizing an acceptable BMP (e.g., AD, OPS, or ATS) is eligible to sell all nutrient loading reductions arising from use of the BMP compared to the threshold (i.e., 80% of the nutrient loading in scenario REF). (See Figure S8 of SI for additional details of nutrient credit calculations).
