Presumably, these systems would generate larger revenues in other states that allow 100%

of the nutrient emissions reductions to be sold as credits. Thus, additional future work should focus on evaluating the economic sustainability of AD, OPS, and ATS under the other states' trading frameworks.

Two other considerations affecting the relative economic appeal of possible dairy waste management strategies include risk and scale. Regarding risk, comparison of NPV distributions (as opposed to mean or median values) reveals that two algae scenarios (OPS, ATS) exhibit small risk. This is evident in the NPV distributions for OPS and ATS (Fig. 3b), whereby each system has small likeli- hood of delivering NPV 0: roughly 10%. Regarding scale, farm size significantly affects expected economic performance. This is be- cause capital costs for digestion systems do not scale linearly with increasing farm size (see SI Section 7.1), and this may also be true for algae cultivation systems. If so, OPS, and ATS systems could be- come even more economically attractive for herd sizes larger than the 100-cow farm selected for this study.

Capital costs ac- counted for initial investments for infrastructure, major facilities, working capital, engineering, and contingencies. Land costs were excluded, because direct land use in all four systems is much smal- ler than the typical farm size in this region (Section 3.2), and it was assumed that sufficient land could be allocated for waste manage- ment from the existing farm area. Annual operational costs ac- counted for energy and materials (electricity, fuel, fertilizer, etc.), labor, capital charges, insurance, and systems maintenance. Addi- tionally, five possible revenue sources were evaluated: (1) sale of bioelectricity back to the grid at $0.07/KW-hr; (2) sale of nutrient credits at $20/lb-N; (3) sale of carbon credits at $0.005/kg CO2; (4) sale of solid digestate as soil amendment at $7.8-15.6/Mg dig- estate; and (5) sale of corn at $234/Mg. (See Supplementary SI for full revenue distributions.) It was assumed that REF could only ac- crue corn revenues, by selling the crop that is produced on the lands where dairy waste is applied as soil amendment. Conversely, OPS and ATS could achieve all types of revenues except sale of corn, because there is no need for land application of either raw or di- gested manure waste. Thus, only AD was expected to achieve all five possible revenue streams, since digestions yield not only bio- electricity, N/CO2 credits, and solid digestate, but also nutrient-rich liquid digestate which is land-applied on fields for corn cultivation.

Fig. 3a summarizes the results of the economic analysis, show- ing net present value (NPV) over an assumed 20-years project life for all waste management strategies (in Panel a). From this figure, OPS, and ATS exhibit economically favorable performances, as indicated by NPV 0. Values were $853,250 for OPS, and $790,280 for ATS. Van Houtven et al. (2012). From these data, both OPS and ATS could be very cost-competitive in the geographic regions where sustainable nutrient credit markets are likely to exist, e.g., Chesa- peake Bay area (Jones et al., 2012; Stephenson and Shabman, 2011; Van Houtven et al., 2012).

The extent to which OPS and ATS economic viability is reliant on nutrient credit trading is a somewhat unexpected outcome of this work. Thus, it is valuable to assess the applicability of existing and emerging nutrient credit market frameworks. Such markets currently exist in Maryland, Pennsylvania, Virginia, and West Vir- ginia, all of which contain watersheds draining to the Chesapeake Bay. Although some elements of the trading programs in each state are very similar, the definition and specific requirements for credit trading do exhibit some differences, including: what pollutants can be traded, permissible trading ratios, eligibility requirements for credit sellers, etc. (Branosky et al., 2011). This variability makes it such that there is as yet no common methodology for defining and quantifying tradable nutrient credits. To make our analysis rel- evant, we patterned our computation after recent work by Ste- phenson and Shabman (2011), using the Maryland and Pennsylvania frameworks as guideline (Branosky et al., 2011; Maryland Department of Agriculture, 2008). We felt that this was appropriate because Maryland offers many options for acceptable

"best management practices" (BMPs), and their nutrient trading framework currently classifies the ATS system as an "innovative" (unproven) BMP. In contrast, Virginia does not currently consider anaerobic digestion or algae cultivation to be acceptable BMPs for nutrient trading.

With respect to specific calculations, we premised our analysis on the assumption that the REF scenario meets all applicable reg- ulations pertaining to nutrient management plans and erosion controls. The nutrient loadings associated with the REF system are then used to define the baseline against which nutrient loadings from the other scenarios can be compared. Per the Pennsylvania nutrient trading framework, a 20% reduction compared to baseline is defined as the threshold for nutrient credit generation (Branosky et al., 2011). As such, a farm utilizing an acceptable BMP (e.g., AD, OPS, or ATS) is eligible to sell all nutrient loading reductions arising from use of the BMP compared to the threshold (i.e., 80% of the nutrient loading in scenario REF). (See Figure S8 of SI for additional details of nutrient credit calculations). We expect that use of the Maryland and Pennsylvania frameworks, though relevant given its flexibility for trying out new BMPs, may result in overly conser- vative estimates of how financially attractive the AD, OPS, and ATS systems are compared to the REF. Presumably, these systems would generate larger revenues in other states that allow 100%

of the nutrient emissions reductions to be sold as credits. Thus, additional future work should focus on evaluating the economic sustainability of AD, OPS, and ATS under the other states' trading frameworks.

Two other considerations affecting the relative economic appeal of possible dairy waste management strategies include risk and scale. Regarding risk, comparison of NPV distributions (as opposed to mean or median values) reveals that two algae scenarios (OPS, ATS) exhibit small risk. This is evident in the NPV distributions for OPS and ATS (Fig. 3b), whereby each system has small likeli- hood of delivering NPV 0: roughly 10%. Regarding scale, farm size significantly affects expected economic performance. This is be- cause capital costs for digestion systems do not scale linearly with increasing farm size (see SI Section 7.1), and this may also be true for algae cultivation systems. If so, OPS, and ATS systems could be- come even more economically attractive for herd sizes larger than the 100-cow farm selected for this study.

35: In evaluating the overall sustainability of possible manure waste nutrient management systems, environmental and eco- nomic performances are important considerations. For the four management strategies evaluated in this study, all three "im- proved" scenarios (AD, OPS, and ATS) were found to be far environ- mentally superior to storage and land application (REF). The direct land use requirement for REF was more than twice as large as for AD, OPS, or ATS. For energy production, REF was the only scenario that resulted in net energy consumption. With respect to economic feasibility, both algae systems (OPS and ATS) exhibit similar per- formance and are preferable to scenario REF and AD. Thus, either algae system offers more favorable land, environmental, and eco- nomic performance.

Still, OPS and ATS systems are unlikely to be profitable on the basis of energy production alone, since they are highly dependent on the price of nutrient credits. This means that farmers may be unwilling to invest in ATS (or even OPS) systems, despite their environmental preferability, since the algae systems may be un- able to achieve their full potential in the absence of a robust nutri- ent credit market. This observation highlights the need for environmental policies which offer financial incentive for environ- mentally responsible technologies, such that economic favorability is better aligned with environmental favorability during on-farm decision-making.

37: This study provides conclusive evidence that adoption of "im- proved" nutrient management strategies will dramatically im- prove the sustainability of manure management on small dairy farms. Addition of digestion with or without algae cultivation makes farm operations net positive in energy production. Use of an algae system, for beneficial recycling of nutrient-rich liquid dig- estate, further improves environmental and economic perfor- mance. Results demonstrate the environmental and economic benefits that could be achieved via integration of waste manage- ment and energy production, but only if economic incentives (e.g., nutrient credits) are in place to motivate environmentally- responsible decision-making.
